summary: Stancil uses Coalesce in San Diego as a lens to argue that the data industry is maturing away from hype and toward process-heavy, often boring work, with AI notably absent. That shift strips away the “main-character” career narrative and forces practitioners to ask whether they’re in the field for the craft or for the promised spotlight. He hopes the community keeps making room for human celebration even as the industry professionalizes and attention moves on.
money_quotes: The problems that data teams deal with aren’t likely to be solved by some fancy flourish of AI, or by a wrapper around ChatGPT.
Our main-character strut is turning into a slump: At Coalesce, several VCs told me they weren’t very excited about any of the early-stage data startups that they’d met recently.
Do I actually want to do any of those things?
What’s special is giving people who are increasingly working in the dark their moment in the light.
key_insight: As data work shifts from hype to legwork, the industry must decide whether it values the craft itself or merely the status it once promised.
